summary:
The Kingdom of Funan (c. 1st–7th centuries AD) was the first major state in mainland Southeast Asia, located in the lower Mekong Delta region of modern Cambodia and southern Vietnam. Funan prospered by controlling the maritime trade routes between India and China — its ports at Oc Eo served as entrepôts where merchants from both great civilizations traded, making Funan extraordinarily cosmopolitan for its time. Archaeological excavations at Oc Eo have uncovered Roman coins, Indian-style jewelry, Chinese bronzes, and Sanskrit inscriptions — physical evidence of the remarkable cultural mixing that defined early Funan.

Funan was one of the first 'Indianized' states of Southeast Asia — adopting Hinduism and (later) Buddhism, Sanskrit as a court language, Indian political concepts including the god-king (devaraja) ideology, Indian architectural styles, and Brahmin priests as administrators and court scholars. This Indianization process was not imposed by conquest but absorbed voluntarily by indigenous Southeast Asian elites who found Indian models of kingship, religion, and administration useful for organizing increasingly complex states. Funan was eventually superseded by the Khmer Chenla kingdom in the 6th–7th centuries, which would eventually develop into the Khmer Empire (Angkor). The pattern of Indian cultural influence combined with indigenous Southeast Asian adaptation that Funan established became the template for Southeast Asian civilization more broadly.
